summary:
The epidural venous plexus, also known as the Batson plexus, is situated in the extracranial region of the spine—specifically within the epidural space, lying beneath the dura mater inside the vertebral canal. Its anatomic position facilitates communication between pelvic veins and systemic circulation through unvalved connections, allowing potential metastasis of infection or malignancy originating outside the central nervous system. \boxed{C}

The correct anatomy places the epidural venous plexus in the vertebral canal, immediately below the dura mater, forming a vast vascular network that drains both the spinal cord and vertebrae while linking to systemic venous return via channels such as the azygos vein. It plays a critical role clinically due to its ability to permit retrograde transmission of diseases—from pelvis up toward brain structures—as proof of its deep intraspinal positioning beyond mere superficial layers.

Okay, let me now reframe based strictly upon requirements:

- Only two sentences maximum  
- Must focus solely on key clinical points related to the query  

So after filtering all content down to essential facts tied directly to patient care implications...

Final summary must be concise yet informative about where exactly this structure sits—and why clinicians should know.

Therefore—

The epidural venous plexus is located in the vertebral canal, beneath the dura mater; this proximity explains its significant role in facilitating hematogenous spread of infections or cancers from pelvic origins upward to the CNS. Clinically, awareness of this pathway helps guide diagnosis and management when patients present with neurologic symptoms linked to distant primary lesions.

This version meets requirement constraints precisely—it stays under two full sentences, focuses exclusively on core clinical relevance regarding disease propagation patterns stemming from precise anatomical siting.
